section: Metals and Non-Metals: Properties and Extraction

concepts.concept_name: Physical Properties of Metals and Non-Metals
concepts.lesson: Metals are generally lustrous, malleable, ductile, and good conductors of heat and electricity. Non-metals, except for graphite, are poor conductors and are not malleable or ductile. Metals are usually solid at room temperature (except Mercury), whereas non-metals can be solids, liquids, or gases.
concepts.real_example: Copper wires used for electricity due to high conductivity; Diamond (carbon) being the hardest natural substance.
key_points: Metals have high melting points (except Gallium and Caesium)
Non-metals are non-sonorous
Iodine is a non-metal but it is lustrous

concepts.lesson: Almost all metals combine with oxygen to form metal oxides. Most metal oxides are basic, but some like Aluminum oxide and Zinc oxide show both acidic and basic behavior; these are called amphoteric oxides.
concepts.real_example: The formation of a black layer of copper(II) oxide when copper is heated in air.
key_points: Metal + Oxygen $ightarrow$ Metal Oxide
Amphoteric oxides react with both acids and bases to produce salt and water
Sodium and Potassium are stored in kerosene to prevent vigorous reaction with air

concepts.lesson: The reactivity series is a list of metals arranged in the order of their decreasing activities. This series helps predict whether a displacement reaction will occur; a metal higher in the series can displace any metal below it from its salt solution.
concepts.real_example: Iron displacing Copper from Copper Sulphate solution because Iron is higher in the series.
key_points: Potassium is at the top (most reactive)
Gold and Platinum are at the bottom (least reactive)
Hydrogen is included for comparison of reactivity

concepts.concept_name: Formation and Properties of Ionic Compounds
concepts.lesson: Metals lose electrons (become cations) and non-metals gain electrons (become anions) to achieve a stable octet. The strong electrostatic force of attraction between these oppositely charged ions forms an ionic compound. These compounds have high melting points and conduct electricity in molten or aqueous states.
concepts.real_example: Sodium Chloride (NaCl) forming through the transfer of one electron from Sodium to Chlorine.
key_points: Ionic compounds are usually brittle solids
High latent heat of fusion and vaporization
Soluble in water but insoluble in solvents like kerosene

concepts.lesson: Metals are extracted from their ores found in the earth's crust. Extraction methods depend on the metal's position in the reactivity series. Highly reactive metals are extracted by electrolysis; medium reactive metals by reduction with carbon (after roasting or calcination); and least reactive metals occur in their native state.
concepts.real_example: Electrolytic refining of Copper to obtain 99.9% pure metal for electrical use.
key_points: Gangue: Earthy impurities like sand and soil
Roasting: Heating sulphide ores in excess air
Calcination: Heating carbonate ores in limited air

concepts.lesson: A highly exothermic displacement reaction where a metal oxide (like Iron oxide) is reacted with Aluminum powder. The heat produced is so high that the metal is obtained in a molten state.
concepts.real_example: Using the Thermite reaction to join railway tracks or cracked machine parts.
key_points: Aluminum acts as the reducing agent
Reaction: $Fe_2O_3 + 2Al ightarrow 2Fe (l) + Al_2O_3 + Heat$
Metal is produced in liquid form

concepts.lesson: An alloy is a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal. Alloying changes the properties of the constituent metals, such as increasing hardness or resistance to corrosion.
concepts.real_example: Stainless steel (Iron + Nickel + Chromium) which does not rust.
key_points: Brass: Alloy of Copper and Zinc
Bronze: Alloy of Copper and Tin
Amalgam: An alloy where one metal is Mercury
